medications.indication_rationale: First-line adrenergic control in thyroid storm — blunts the catecholamine-driven tachyarrhythmia and hypermetabolism and (at high dose) reduces peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. This is an emergency in-hospital/paramedic treatment (typically intravenous with cardiac monitoring); the telehealth AI Doctor does NOT administer it but recognises the emergency and escalates so it can be given. It is one of the patient's own ceased medications.
contraindications_in_this_case: Use cautiously if overt high-output cardiac failure supervenes — requires titration under continuous monitoring in a critical-care setting

medications.indication_rationale: Blocks new thyroid hormone synthesis; the definitive anti-thyroid arm of storm treatment and the medication the patient abruptly ceased. Restarting/definitive dosing is an in-hospital decision; the AI Doctor's role is recognition and escalation, not prescribing.

Reduces peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 and covers possible relative adrenal insufficiency in the stressed state; a standard component of in-hospital thyroid-storm therapy. Administered in hospital, not by the AI Doctor.

Blocks release of preformed thyroid hormone; MUST be given at least an hour AFTER the thionamide (giving iodine first can fuel hormone synthesis). An in-hospital, sequenced treatment — noted here for completeness of the definitive pathway, not administered via telehealth.
Appropriate antipyretic to accompany active external cooling for the hyperpyrexia. External cooling is the primary measure; paracetamol is the preferred pharmacological antipyretic here.

Contraindicated in thyroid storm — salicylates displace thyroid hormone from thyroxine-binding globulin, raising free hormone levels and worsening the crisis. Using aspirin to bring the fever down is a dangerous error of commission; use external cooling plus paracetamol instead.
contraindications_in_this_case: Displaces thyroid hormone from binding proteins and increases free T3/T4 — worsens thyrotoxicosis

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Never abruptly stop anti-thyroid medication; take carbimazole daily as prescribed and contact the treating team before any change rather than ceasing on your own.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Abrupt cessation of thionamide therapy was the key precipitant of this life-threatening crisis; continuous control prevents recurrent hormone surges.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Seek early review during any intercurrent illness (such as a chest infection), which can destabilise an overactive thyroid.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Infection is a recognised trigger of decompensation; early attention to precipitants reduces the risk of another storm.

patient_education_points.point: Her racing heart, high fever and confusion are the physical result of a dangerous surge of thyroid hormone — this is a medical emergency, not a panic attack.
The ambulance and hospital team will slow her heart, cool her down, and give medicines to stop her thyroid pouring out hormone and to block its effects.
Bring her old carbimazole and propranolol packets to hospital so the team knows exactly what she was prescribed and when she stopped.
Once she recovers, the most important thing is to keep taking the thyroid medication every day and never stop it suddenly.

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency transport now; urgent endocrinology and intensive-care review on arrival
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: airway/breathing/circulation and cardiac rhythm, temperature and conscious state; thyroid function and precipitant work-up; endocrinology-led definitive control and a relapse-prevention plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any collapse, loss of consciousness, or cessation of normal breathing before the ambulance arrives — this is a further emergency

must_include_items: Recognise a life-threatening hypermetabolic emergency (do not anchor solely on sepsis or label it a panic attack)
Call 000 for an emergency (Priority 1) ambulance without delay
Coach the carer through the patient-doable measures: external cooling (cool wet cloths to groin and armpits), a quiet low-stimulation environment, and safe supervision without forcible restraint
Recognise that definitive treatment (beta-blocker, anti-thyroid drug, iodine, steroid, cooling) requires hospital care and cannot be given via telehealth
Elicit the key history: known overactive thyroid, abrupt cessation of anti-thyroid tablets, and the recent infection
acceptable_alternatives: Advising the carer to have the ceased carbimazole/propranolol packets ready for the paramedics
Co-flagging the concurrent infection to the ambulance/ED as the likely precipitant
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate to 000 / treating this as non-urgent
Anchoring on the chest infection and missing the thyroid storm
Missing the abrupt anti-thyroid medication cessation as the precipitant
errors_of_commission: Recommending aspirin or another salicylate to reduce the fever
Labelling the agitation and hallucinations as a primary panic attack or psychiatric episode and under-triaging
Advising home management or waiting to see the GP
Attempting to give or dose emergency thyroid-storm drugs remotely, or advising physical restraint of the agitated patient
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: AUTOMATIC FAIL if the emergency is not recognised and 000 escalation is not given, or if a salicylate antipyretic is recommended. A pass requires emergency escalation PLUS correct carer-doable cooling/environment advice.
